name: Dead Sea Scrolls
shortDescription: A collection of ancient Jewish religious texts discovered in caves near the Dead Sea, containing some of the oldest known surviving copies of biblical texts.
description: The Dead Sea Scrolls are a collection of approximately 981 different texts discovered between 1946 and 1956 in eleven caves near the ancient settlement of Qumran, on the northwest shore of the Dead Sea. These texts are of great historical, religious, and linguistic significance because they include some of the oldest known surviving copies of Biblical texts, along with apocryphal and pseudepigraphal works, and previously unknown Jewish writings. Written primarily in Hebrew, Aramaic, and Greek, the scrolls provide invaluable insight into Jewish thought and practice during the Second Temple period (roughly 516 BCE to 70 CE). The scrolls include fragments of nearly every book of the Hebrew Bible, offering variants and confirming the general accuracy of the Masoretic Text. Beyond the biblical fragments, the collection includes commentaries (pesharim), sectarian writings associated with the Essenes (a Jewish sect), rules of community, war scrolls, hymns, and liturgical texts. These non-biblical texts shed light on the diverse religious and social landscape of Judaism at the time, including beliefs about angels, the messiah, and the afterlife. The discovery of the Dead Sea Scrolls has revolutionized our understanding of the development of both Judaism and Christianity, providing crucial context for the emergence of early Christian thought. The scrolls continue to be studied and debated by scholars worldwide, revealing new insights into the rich and complex history of ancient Jewish culture and its lasting influence.

sources.title: The Dead Sea Scrolls: A Very Short Introduction
